ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for a multi-bowl rice set meal with can drinks. Mapped to broader 'rice dish' (FOODON_00002678) as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for a Singaporean rice bowl set meal. A000E (rice-based composite dish) is used as the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor. Exact code should be confirmed against the full FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept maps precisely to a multi-bowl rice set meal; left null to avoid fabrication.

main_ingredients: protein topping (variable: chicken, tofu, egg, or fish)

serving.description: 1 rice bowl (approximately 300g cooked rice with toppings)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard rice bowl serving is approximately 300g, comprising steamed rice with a protein topping and vegetables, as typically served in a Singaporean rice bowl set.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Foodo — Rice Bowl (composite dish, mixed protein and vegetable toppings)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database for a rice bowl dish (composite, mixed toppings). Input record carries HPB provenance at 0.95 confidence across all macros and micronutrients. Values preserved as supplied. Saturated fat not reported in the HPB source for this entry; estimated at 1.2g/100g based on typical rice bowl composition (lean protein + vegetable toppings) using USDA FoodData Central analogous components. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and water are not reported in the HPB source for this entry and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — White rice, boiled (analogous estimate for rice bowl dish)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a composite Singaporean rice bowl set meal. GI of 64 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled white rice (GI ~64, medium category), which is the dominant carbohydrate component of this dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (64 × 25.19g × 300g/100) / 100 = (64 × 75.57g) / 100 ≈ 48.4 / 100 × serving fraction. GL per 300g serving = 48.4; per 100g reference GL = 16.1. For the template field glycemic_load_per_serving, the 300g serving GL is reported as 48.4, rounded to 48.4. Corrected: GL per serving (300g) = (64 × (25.19 × 3)) / 100 = (64 × 75.57) / 100 = 48.36 ≈ 48.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231mg/100g; partially offset by potassium 327mg/100g (Na:K ratio < 1, favourable for DASH criteria)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced sauce portions when ordering this rice bowl set, as sauces are the primary sodium contributor; the favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio (327mg vs 231mg per 100g) supports blood pressure management when sauce is controlled.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 25.19g/100g with dietary fibre 3.7g/100g and protein 6.5g/100g moderating glycaemic response; estimated GI 64 (medium)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should choose brown rice or mixed-grain rice over white rice when available to lower the glycaemic index, and limit portion size to one bowl (approximately 300g) to keep glycaemic load within a manageable range; the dietary fibre and protein content help blunt post-meal glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 4.21g/100g with estimated saturated fat ~1.2g/100g; cholesterol 0mg/100g; dietary fibre 3.7g/100g supports LDL reduction
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This rice bowl set is low in saturated fat (~1.2g/100g) and contains no cholesterol, making it a heart-friendly option; the dietary fibre at 3.7g/100g contributes to LDL cholesterol reduction, and individuals with cardiovascular disease should request lean protein toppings (chicken breast, tofu, or fish) and minimal sauce to keep sodium and saturated fat intake low.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Dietary fibre 3.7g/100g supports gut microbiome immune regulation; estimated saturated fat ~1.2g/100g is well below the 5g pro-inflammatory threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should confirm the protein source and request chicken, tofu, or egg over red meat to avoid arachidonic acid loading, while the high dietary fibre content supports anti-inflammatory gut microbiome activity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Rice Bowl Bestie is a Singaporean rice bowl set meal consisting of two customisable rice bowls paired with two can drinks, marketed as a value combo for shared or individual dining. Each rice bowl typically comprises steamed white rice (or optionally brown or mixed-grain rice) topped with a protein of choice — commonly chicken, tofu, egg, or fish — alongside cooked vegetables and a flavoured sauce. The dish is a staple of Singapore's casual dining scene, reflecting the multicultural food culture of the island where rice-based meals are central to daily eating patterns across Chinese, Malay, Indian, and Western-influenced cuisines. Nutritionally, the dish provides 164 kcal per 100g, with a moderate carbohydrate content of 25.19g, a meaningful protein contribution of 6.5g, and a relatively low fat content of 4.21g. The dietary fibre content of 3.7g per 100g is notable and supports satiety, gut health, and glycaemic modulation. Sodium is 231mg per 100g, which is borderline but offset by a favourable potassium level of 327mg per 100g. The estimated glycaemic index of 64 (medium) reflects the white rice base, though the fibre and protein content moderate the post-meal glucose response. Clinically, this dish is suitable for individuals managing hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and cardiovascular disease when portion size and sauce quantity are controlled. It is best consumed as a lunch or dinner meal and is widely available across Singapore's hawker centres, food courts, and casual dining restaurants.

confidence_reason: All core macros and key micronutrients sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database with 0.95 confidence as recorded in the input provenance. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous components (lean protein + vegetable rice bowl) and clearly documented. GI estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using boiled white rice as the closest analogous food, with the estimation basis fully documented.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.2g/100g) is an estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central analogous components for a rice bowl with lean protein and vegetable toppings; the HPB source does not report saturated fat for this entry. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this composite rice bowl dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database as of 2026-04-16. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as reported by HPB; confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo source. 4. The food name 'Rice Bowl Bestie' is a promotional/marketing name; the canonical food name has been normalised to 'Rice Bowl Set (2 Bowls with Can Drinks)' for database integrity. 5. The input record lists primary_cuisine as 'Western' but the dish is a Singaporean rice bowl set sold in Singapore; cuisine has been corrected to 'Singaporean' based on regional context and HPB provenance. 6. GI of 64 is a conservative analogous estimate based on boiled white rice (University of Sydney GI Database); actual GI may be lower due to fibre and protein content of the composite dish. 7. Glycemic load per 300g serving = 48.4 (calculated as GI × available carbohydrate in serving / 100 = 64 × 75.57 / 100). 8. Halal status is not confirmed; the dish may contain pork-derived sauces depending on the vendor. 9. Allergen profile is indicative only; actual allergens depend on the specific protein topping and sauce used by the vendor.
